docs.title: AgentPair
docs.text:
Agent-to-agent pair programming between Claude and Codex.

AgentPair orchestrates pair programming sessions between AI agents. One agent works on the task while the other reviews, iterating until completion or max iterations reached.

Inspired by loop, AgentPair is a Go implementation with the same core concepts: paired agent execution, bridge-based communication, and persistent run state.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Paired mode — Claude and Codex work together; one implements, the other reviews
  • Single-agent mode — Run --claude-only or --codex-only for simpler tasks
  • Bridge communication — JSONL-based messaging with SHA256 deduplication
  • MCP integration — Agents communicate via Model Context Protocol tools
  • Review modes — claude, codex, or claudex (both review)
  • tmux support — Side-by-side terminal panes for watching agents work
  • Git worktree isolation — Each run can use an isolated worktree
  • Session persistence — Resume runs from any state
  • Live dashboard — Monitor active runs in real-time
  • Auto-update — Self-updating binary

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
# Paired session (Codex works, Claude reviews)
agentpair --prompt "Implement a REST API for user management"

# With side-by-side tmux view
agentpair --tmux --prompt "Add OAuth2 authentication"

# Single-agent mode
agentpair --claude-only "Refactor the logging system"

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                AgentPair Loop              │
│                                            │
│   ┌─────────┐  bridge.jsonl  ┌─────────┐   │
│   │  Claude │<──────────────>│  Codex  │   │
│   │  (work) │                │(review) │   │
│   └────┬────┘                └────┬────┘   │
│        │                          │        │
│        └──────────┬───────────────┘        │
│                   │                        │
│              State Machine                 │
│   init → working → reviewing → complete    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  1. Primary agent (Codex by default) works on the task
  2. Secondary agent (Claude by default) reviews the work
  3. Agents communicate through bridge messages
  4. Loop continues until DONE/PASS signals or max iterations

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Installation — Get agentpair running
  • Quick Start — Your first paired session
  • Architecture — How it all fits together

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: AgentPair is organized into focused packages with clear responsibilities.

docs.title: Package Structure
docs.text:
github.com/plexusone/agentpair/
├── cmd/agentpair/          # CLI entry point
├── internal/
│   ├── agent/              # Agent interface and implementations
│   │   ├── claude/         # Claude CLI wrapper
│   │   └── codex/          # Codex App Server client
│   ├── bridge/             # Agent-to-agent messaging
│   ├── loop/               # Orchestration state machine
│   ├── run/                # Run persistence
│   ├── review/             # Signal parsing
│   ├── tmux/               # Terminal multiplexer
│   ├── worktree/           # Git worktree management
│   ├── dashboard/          # Live monitoring UI
│   ├── config/             # Configuration handling
│   ├── logger/             # Structured logging
│   └── update/             # Auto-update mechanism
└── pkg/jsonl/              # JSONL utilities

docs.title: Agent Interface
docs.text:
The Agent interface defines how AgentPair interacts with AI agents:

type Agent interface {
    Name() string
    Start(ctx context.Context) error
    Execute(ctx context.Context, msgs []*bridge.Message) (*Result, error)
    Stop(ctx context.Context) error
    SessionID() string
    IsRunning() bool
    SetMCPServerAddr(addr string)
}

Two implementations exist:

  • Claude — Wraps the claude CLI using NDJSON protocol
  • Codex — Connects to Codex App Server via JSON-RPC 2.0

docs.title: Bridge
docs.text:
The bridge handles message passing between agents:

┌─────────┐                      ┌─────────┐
│  Claude │                      │  Codex  │
└────┬────┘                      └────┬────┘
     │                                │
     │  send_to_agent("codex", ...)   │
     ├───────────────────────────────►│
     │                                │
     │◄───────────────────────────────┤
     │  send_to_agent("claude", ...)  │
     │                                │
     ▼                                ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            bridge.jsonl                  │
│  {"id":"abc","from":"claude","to":"codex"...}
│  {"id":"def","from":"codex","to":"claude"...}
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Key features:

  • JSONL storage — Messages persisted to disk
  • SHA256 deduplication — Prevents duplicate processing
  • MCP server — Exposes bridge tools to agents

docs.title: Loop
docs.text:
The orchestration loop manages the agent lifecycle:

type Loop struct {
    config    *config.Config
    run       *run.Run
    primary   agent.Agent
    secondary agent.Agent
    machine   *Machine
}

Main flow:

  1. Start both agents
  2. Execute primary agent with pending messages
  3. Drain bridge, execute secondary agent
  4. Check for completion signals
  5. Repeat until done or max iterations

docs.title: Run Manager
docs.text:
Persists run state to disk:

~/.agentpair/runs/{repo-id}/{run-id}/
├── manifest.json      # Run metadata
├── bridge.jsonl       # Agent messages
└── transcript.jsonl   # Audit log

The manifest tracks:

  • Run ID and prompt
  • Agent configuration
  • Current state and iteration
  • Session IDs for resume
  • Timestamps

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: Package Purpose github.com/spf13/cobra CLI framework github.com/coder/websocket WebSocket client/server github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk MCP SDK go.yaml.in/yaml/v3 YAML configuration

docs.title: Why JSONL for Bridge?
docs.text:
• Simple, append-only format
  • Human-readable for debugging
  • Easy to parse and stream
  • Survives crashes (no corruption)

docs.title: Why SHA256 for Deduplication?
docs.text:
• Content-addressable messages
  • Deterministic IDs across restarts
  • No coordination needed between agents

docs.title: Why Separate Agent Implementations?
docs.text:
• Different protocols (NDJSON vs JSON-RPC)
  • Different session management
  • Different tool capabilities
  • Easier to add new agents

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• State Machine — Loop state transitions
  • Bridge — Message format and tools
  • MCP Integration — Model Context Protocol

docs.title: Bridge
docs.text: The bridge enables agent-to-agent communication through a JSONL file with SHA256 deduplication.

docs.title: Message Format
docs.text:
Each message is a JSON object:

{
  "id": "sha256-abc123...",
  "run_id": 1,
  "type": "task",
  "from": "claude",
  "to": "codex",
  "content": "Please implement the login form validation",
  "signal": "",
  "timestamp": "2024-03-28T10:00:00Z"
}

docs.title: Fields
docs.text: Field Type Description id string SHA256 hash of content for deduplication run_id int Associated run ID type string Message type (see below) from string Sender agent name to string Target agent name (or empty for broadcast) content string Message content signal string Control signal (DONE, PASS, FAIL) timestamp string ISO 8601 timestamp

docs.title: Message Types
docs.text: Type Description task Initial task or follow-up work request result Work output from an agent review Review feedback signal Control signal (DONE, PASS, FAIL) chat Free-form discussion

docs.title: Signals
docs.text: Signal Meaning DONE Agent has completed the task PASS Reviewer approves the work FAIL Reviewer rejects the work

docs.title: Deduplication
docs.text:
Messages are deduplicated using SHA256:

func GenerateID(msgType MessageType, from, to, content string) string {
    h := sha256.New()
    h.Write([]byte(string(msgType)))
    h.Write([]byte(from))
    h.Write([]byte(to))
    h.Write([]byte(content))
    return hex.EncodeToString(h.Sum(nil))
}

This ensures:

  • Same message content → same ID
  • Duplicate sends are ignored
  • Safe for retries and restarts

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
Messages are stored in bridge.jsonl:

~/.agentpair/runs/{repo-id}/{run-id}/bridge.jsonl

Format is newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON):

{"id":"abc...","type":"task","from":"claude",...}
{"id":"def...","type":"result","from":"codex",...}
{"id":"ghi...","type":"review","from":"claude",...}

docs.title: Send
docs.text:
func (b *Bridge) Send(ctx context.Context, msg *Message) (bool, error) {
    // Check for duplicate
    if b.seen[msg.ID] {
        return false, nil  // Already sent
    }

    // Mark as seen and append to storage
    b.seen[msg.ID] = true
    return true, b.storage.Append(msg)
}

docs.title: Drain
docs.text:
func (b *Bridge) Drain(ctx context.Context, target string, seenIDs map[string]bool) ([]*Message, error) {
    all, _ := b.storage.ReadAll()

    var messages []*Message
    for _, msg := range all {
        if msg.IsForAgent(target) && !seenIDs[msg.ID] {
            messages = append(messages, msg)
        }
    }
    return messages, nil
}

docs.title: Status
docs.text:
type Status struct {
    TotalMessages int
    ByAgent       map[string]int
    ByType        map[MessageType]int
    HasDoneSignal bool
    PassCount     int
    FailCount     int
}

docs.title: Example Flow
docs.text:
1. Claude sends task to Codex: 

    {"type":"task","from":"claude","to":"codex","content":"Implement login form"}

  2. Codex receives and works: 

    {"type":"result","from":"codex","to":"claude","content":"Added LoginForm.tsx..."}

  3. Claude reviews: 

    {"type":"review","from":"claude","to":"codex","content":"Looks good, minor fix needed..."}

  4. Codex fixes and signals done: 

    {"type":"signal","from":"codex","signal":"DONE","content":"Fixed and ready"}

  5. Claude approves: 

    {"type":"signal","from":"claude","signal":"PASS","content":"Approved"}

docs.title: Viewing Bridge Status
docs.text:
# All runs
agentpair bridge

# Specific run
agentpair bridge --run-id 1

Output:

Run #1 Bridge Status:
  Total Messages: 12
  Done Signal: true
  Pass Count: 1
  Fail Count: 0
  By Agent:
    claude: 5
    codex: 7

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• MCP Integration — How agents access bridge tools
  • Bridge Messages Reference — Full message specification

docs.title: MCP Integration
docs.text: AgentPair exposes bridge functionality to agents via the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
MCP provides a standard way for AI models to interact with external tools. AgentPair runs an MCP server that gives agents access to bridge communication tools.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  AgentPair                       │
│                                                  │
│  ┌─────────┐         ┌─────────────┐            │
│  │  Claude │◄───────►│  MCP Server │            │
│  └─────────┘   MCP   │             │            │
│                      │  - send_to_agent         │
│  ┌─────────┐         │  - receive_messages      │
│  │  Codex  │◄───────►│  - bridge_status         │
│  └─────────┘   MCP   └──────┬──────┘            │
│                             │                    │
│                             ▼                    │
│                      ┌─────────────┐            │
│                      │   Bridge    │            │
│                      └─────────────┘            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: send_to_agent
docs.text:
Send a message to another agent through the bridge.

Parameters:

Name Type Required Description to string Yes Target agent name (claude or codex) message_type string Yes Type: task, result, review, signal, chat content string Yes Message content signal string No Signal value: DONE, PASS, or FAIL

Example:

{
  "to": "codex",
  "message_type": "task",
  "content": "Please implement input validation for the email field"
}

Response:

Message sent: id=abc123...

Or if duplicate:

Message duplicate (already sent): id=abc123...

docs.title: receive_messages
docs.text:
Receive pending messages from the bridge.

Parameters:

Name Type Required Description agent string Yes Agent name to receive messages for since_id string No Only return messages after this ID

Example:

{
  "agent": "claude",
  "since_id": "abc123..."
}

Response:

[1] from=codex type=result
Here's my implementation of the email validation...

[2] from=codex type=signal signal=DONE
Task completed successfully.

docs.title: bridge_status
docs.text:
Get the current status of the bridge.

Parameters: None

Response:

Bridge Status:
  Total Messages: 15
  Done Signal: true
  Pass Count: 1
  Fail Count: 0
  Messages by Agent:
    claude: 7
    codex: 8
  Messages by Type:
    task: 3
    result: 5
    review: 4
    signal: 3

docs.title: Server Implementation
docs.text:
The MCP server is implemented using the official modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk:

import "github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk/mcp"

func NewServer(bridge *Bridge) *Server {
    s := &Server{bridge: bridge}

    s.server = mcp.NewServer(&mcp.Implementation{
        Name:    "agentpair-bridge",
        Version: "1.0.0",
    }, nil)

    // Register tools
    mcp.AddTool(s.server, &mcp.Tool{
        Name:        "send_to_agent",
        Description: "Send a message to another agent",
    }, s.handleSendToAgent)

    return s
}

docs.title: Transport
docs.text: The MCP server supports multiple transports:

docs.title: stdio
docs.text:
Standard input/output for subprocess communication:

server.ListenAndServe(ctx, "stdio")

docs.title: TCP
docs.text:
Socket-based for network access:

server.ListenAndServe(ctx, ":9100")

docs.title: Agent Configuration
docs.text:
Agents receive the MCP server address via configuration:

agent.SetMCPServerAddr("localhost:9100")

The agent then connects to this server to access bridge tools.

docs.title: Tool Flow
docs.text:
1. Agent starts — Connects to MCP server
  2. Agent lists tools — Gets send_to_agent, receive_messages, bridge_status
  3. Agent sends message — Calls send_to_agent tool
  4. AgentPair routes — Message stored in bridge.jsonl
  5. Other agent receives — Calls receive_messages tool
  6. Message delivered — Agent processes and responds

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
MCP tools return errors in the result:

func errorResult(text string) *mcp.CallToolResult {
    return &mcp.CallToolResult{
        Content: []mcp.Content{
            &mcp.TextContent{Text: text},
        },
        IsError: true,
    }
}

Agents see these as tool execution failures and can retry or handle appropriately.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Bridge — Message format and deduplication
  • Architecture — System overview

docs.title: State Machine
docs.text: The loop state machine controls the flow of agent execution.

docs.title: States
docs.text: State Description init Run created, agents starting working Primary agent executing task reviewing Secondary agent reviewing work complete Task finished successfully failed Error or max iterations reached

docs.title: Valid Transitions
docs.text: From To Trigger init working Agents started successfully init failed Agent failed to start working reviewing Primary agent finished iteration working complete Primary agent signaled DONE working failed Agent error or max iterations reviewing working Review requires more work reviewing complete Review passed (PASS signal) reviewing failed Unrecoverable review failure

docs.title: Completion Conditions
docs.text:
The loop completes when:

  1. DONE signal — Agent outputs the done signal (default: DONE)
  2. PASS signal — Reviewing agent approves with PASS
  3. Consensus — In claudex mode, both agents signal completion

docs.title: Failure Conditions
docs.text:
The loop fails when:

  1. Max iterations — Reached --max-iterations limit
  2. Agent error — Agent process crashed or returned error
  3. FAIL signal — Agent explicitly fails with FAIL
  4. Context cancelled — User interrupted (Ctrl+C)

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
type State string

const (
    StateInit      State = "init"
    StateWorking   State = "working"
    StateReviewing State = "reviewing"
    StateComplete  State = "complete"
    StateFailed    State = "failed"
)

type Machine struct {
    current State
    history []State
}

func (m *Machine) Transition(newState State) error {
    if !m.canTransition(newState) {
        return &InvalidTransitionError{From: m.current, To: newState}
    }
    m.history = append(m.history, newState)
    m.current = newState
    return nil
}

docs.title: Run State Mapping
docs.text:
Loop states map to run states for persistence:

Loop State Run State init submitted working working reviewing reviewing complete completed failed failed

docs.title: Iteration Flow
docs.text:
Each iteration follows this pattern:

1. Check state is not terminal
2. Increment iteration counter
3. Drain messages for primary agent
4. Execute primary agent
5. Process result (check signals)
6. If not done, transition to reviewing
7. Drain messages for secondary agent
8. Execute secondary agent
9. Process review result
10. Transition back to working or complete

docs.title: Signal Detection
docs.text:
Signals are detected in agent output:

type Result struct {
    Output string
    Done   bool  // DONE signal found
    Pass   bool  // PASS signal found
    Fail   bool  // FAIL signal found
}

The review package parses agent output for these signals.

docs.title: Resuming
docs.text:
When resuming a run, the state machine:

  1. Loads the last known state from manifest
  2. Converts run state to loop state
  3. Continues from that point
func FromRunState(rs run.State) State {
    switch rs {
    case run.StateSubmitted:
        return StateInit
    case run.StateWorking:
        return StateWorking
    case run.StateReviewing:
        return StateReviewing
    case run.StateCompleted:
        return StateComplete
    default:
        return StateFailed
    }
}

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Bridge — How agents communicate
  • Paired Sessions — Running the full loop

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: AgentPair can be configured via command-line flags or a config file. CLI flags always take precedence.

docs.title: Config File
docs.text:
Create ~/.agentpair/config.yaml:

# Primary worker agent: claude or codex
agent: codex

# Maximum loop iterations before stopping
max_iterations: 20

# Review mode: claude, codex, or claudex
review_mode: claudex

# Custom done signal to look for
done_signal: DONE

# Proof requirements for task verification
proof: ""

# Enable tmux side-by-side view
use_tmux: false

# Enable git worktree isolation
use_worktree: false

# Enable verbose logging
verbose: false

# Maximum run duration
timeout: 2h

JSON format is also supported (~/.agentpair/config.json).

docs.title: CLI Flags
docs.text:
Flags override config file values:

agentpair --agent claude --max-iterations 10 --prompt "Task"

docs.title: All Flags
docs.text: Flag Short Default Description --prompt -p Task prompt --agent -a codex Primary worker (claude/codex) --max-iterations -m 20 Maximum loop iterations --proof Proof/verification command --review claudex Review mode --done DONE Custom done signal --claude-only false Single-agent Claude mode --codex-only false Single-agent Codex mode --tmux false Use tmux panes --worktree false Use git worktree --run-id 0 Resume by run ID --session Resume by session ID --verbose -v false Verbose output

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: Environment variables are not currently supported. Use the config file or CLI flags.

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
AgentPair stores data in ~/.agentpair/:

~/.agentpair/
├── config.yaml           # Global configuration
└── runs/
    └── {repo-id}/
        └── {run-id}/
            ├── manifest.json    # Run metadata
            ├── bridge.jsonl     # Agent messages
            └── transcript.jsonl # Audit log

docs.title: Repo ID
docs.text:
The repo ID is derived from the Git remote URL or directory path:

  • github.com/user/repo → github-user-repo
  • /home/user/myproject → home-user-myproject

docs.title: CI/CD Pipeline
docs.text:
agent: codex
max_iterations: 50
review_mode: claudex
verbose: true
timeout: 4h

docs.title: Local Development
docs.text:
agent: claude
max_iterations: 10
use_tmux: true
verbose: false

docs.title: Safety-Focused
docs.text:
agent: codex
max_iterations: 5
review_mode: claudex
use_worktree: true
proof: "go test ./... && golangci-lint run"

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Architecture — Understand the system design
  • Review Modes — Choose the right review strategy

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before installing AgentPair, ensure you have:

  • Go 1.21+ — Required for building from source
  • claude CLI — Claude Code installed and logged in
  • codex CLI — OpenAI Codex installed and logged in

Optional:

  • tmux — For side-by-side agent view
  • Git — For worktree isolation feature

docs.title: Install with Go
docs.text:
go install github.com/plexusone/agentpair@latest

Ensure $GOPATH/bin is in your PATH:

export PATH="$GOPATH/bin:$PATH"

docs.title: Build from Source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/plexusone/agentpair
cd agentpair
go build -o agentpair ./cmd/agentpair

Move to a directory in your PATH:

mv agentpair ~/.local/bin/

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text: agentpair version

docs.title: Claude CLI
docs.text:
Install Claude Code following the official documentation:

# Verify claude is working
claude --version

docs.title: Codex CLI
docs.text:
Install OpenAI Codex following the official documentation:

# Verify codex is working
codex --version

Safety Note

AgentPair runs agents in auto-approve mode by default. Consider running inside a VM or container for safety. The agents can execute commands and modify files.

docs.title: tmux (Optional)
docs.text:
For the side-by-side view feature:

macOSUbuntu/DebianFedora 
brew install tmux

sudo apt install tmux

sudo dnf install tmux

Verify:

tmux -V

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Quick Start — Run your first paired session
  • Configuration — Customize AgentPair settings

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide walks you through your first AgentPair session.

docs.title: Your First Paired Session
docs.text:
Navigate to a project directory and run:

cd ~/my-project
agentpair --prompt "Add input validation to the user registration form"

AgentPair will:

  1. Create a new run with a unique ID
  2. Start Codex as the primary worker
  3. Start Claude as the reviewer
  4. Execute the task in a loop until completion

docs.title: Understanding the Output
docs.text:
2024/03/28 10:00:00 INFO starting agentpair version=dev
2024/03/28 10:00:00 INFO created run run_id=1 prompt="Add input validation..." agent=codex review_mode=claudex
2024/03/28 10:00:00 INFO starting paired-agent mode primary=codex secondary=claude
2024/03/28 10:00:01 INFO iteration started iteration=1 state=working
...
2024/03/28 10:05:00 INFO run complete iterations=3 state=completed

docs.title: Using tmux Mode
docs.text:
For a visual side-by-side view of both agents working:

agentpair --tmux --prompt "Implement user authentication"

This creates a tmux session with:

  • Left pane: Claude
  • Right pane: Codex
  • Bottom pane: Status dashboard

Attach to watch:

tmux attach -t agentpair-myproject-1

docs.title: Single-Agent Mode
docs.text:
For simpler tasks, use one agent:

# Claude only
agentpair --claude-only "Fix the typo in README.md"

# Codex only
agentpair --codex-only "Add unit tests for utils.go"

docs.title: Specifying Proof Requirements
docs.text:
Use --proof to tell agents how to verify their work:

agentpair --prompt "Add caching to the API" --proof "Run 'go test ./...' and ensure all tests pass"

docs.title: Viewing the Dashboard
docs.text:
Monitor all active runs:

agentpair dashboard

docs.title: Checking Bridge Status
docs.text:
See message flow between agents:

# All runs
agentpair bridge

# Specific run
agentpair bridge --run-id 1

docs.title: Feature Implementation
docs.text:
agentpair --tmux --prompt "Implement OAuth2 login with Google" \
  --proof "Run the app and verify login works"

docs.title: Bug Fix
docs.text:
agentpair --prompt "Fix the race condition in cache.go" \
  --proof "Run 'go test -race ./...' with no races detected"

docs.title: Code Review
docs.text: agentpair --claude-only "Review the changes in the last 3 commits for security issues"

docs.title: Refactoring
docs.text:
agentpair --worktree --prompt "Refactor the database layer to use repository pattern" \
  --proof "All existing tests must pass"

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Configuration — Customize default settings
  • Paired Sessions — Deep dive into paired mode
  • Bridge — Understand agent communication

docs.title: Paired Sessions
docs.text: Paired mode is the default in AgentPair. One agent works while the other reviews.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Paired Session                        │
│                                                          │
│   Primary (Codex)              Secondary (Claude)        │
│   ┌─────────────┐              ┌─────────────┐          │
│   │   Working   │──────────────│  Reviewing  │          │
│   └──────┬──────┘              └──────┬──────┘          │
│          │                            │                  │
│          │     Bridge Messages        │                  │
│          └────────────────────────────┘                  │
│                                                          │
│   Iteration 1: Codex implements, Claude reviews          │
│   Iteration 2: Codex fixes issues, Claude re-reviews     │
│   Iteration 3: Both signal DONE/PASS → Complete          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Starting a Paired Session
docs.text:
# Default: Codex works, Claude reviews
agentpair --prompt "Implement user authentication"

# Claude works, Codex reviews
agentpair --agent claude --prompt "Implement user authentication"

docs.title: Review Modes
docs.text:
Control who reviews with --review:

Mode Behavior claude Only Claude reviews codex Only Codex reviews claudex Both review; consensus required 
# Only Claude reviews Codex's work
agentpair --review claude --prompt "Add API endpoints"

# Both review (default)
agentpair --review claudex --prompt "Implement OAuth"

docs.title: Consensus Mode (claudex)
docs.text:
In claudex mode:

  1. Primary agent completes work
  2. Both agents review in parallel
  3. Both must signal PASS for completion
  4. If either signals FAIL, work continues

docs.title: Proof Requirements
docs.text:
Use --proof to specify verification criteria:

agentpair --prompt "Add input validation" \
  --proof "Run 'npm test' and ensure all tests pass"

The proof requirement is passed to agents so they know how to verify their work.

docs.title: Iteration Limits
docs.text:
Control maximum iterations:

# Stop after 10 iterations
agentpair --max-iterations 10 --prompt "Complex refactoring task"

Default is 20 iterations.

docs.title: Custom Done Signal
docs.text:
Change the completion signal:

agentpair --done "COMPLETE" --prompt "Task"

Agents should output this signal when they're done.

docs.title: Feature Development
docs.text:
agentpair --tmux --prompt "Implement a REST API for blog posts" \
  --proof "Run 'go test ./... && curl localhost:8080/posts'" \
  --max-iterations 15

Flow:

  1. Iteration 1: Codex creates basic API structure
  2. Iteration 2: Claude reviews, requests error handling
  3. Iteration 3: Codex adds error handling
  4. Iteration 4: Claude reviews, requests tests
  5. Iteration 5: Codex adds tests
  6. Iteration 6: Claude reviews, signals PASS
  7. Complete: Both agents satisfied

docs.title: Bug Fix
docs.text:
agentpair --prompt "Fix the race condition in cache.go" \
  --proof "Run 'go test -race ./...' with no races" \
  --agent claude  # Claude is better at concurrency

docs.title: Code Review Focus
docs.text:
agentpair --prompt "Review security of auth module" \
  --review claudex \
  --max-iterations 5

docs.title: Watch in Real-Time
docs.text:
# With tmux
agentpair --tmux --prompt "Task"

# Attach to session
tmux attach -t agentpair-myrepo-1

docs.title: Check Status
docs.text:
# Dashboard
agentpair dashboard

# Bridge status
agentpair bridge --run-id 1

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Be specific with prompts — Clear prompts lead to faster convergence
  2. Include proof requirements — Helps agents know when they're done
  3. Use appropriate iterations — Complex tasks need more iterations
  4. Monitor initial runs — Watch with --tmux until you trust the flow
  5. Start small — Test with simple tasks before complex ones

docs.title: Agents Not Converging
docs.text:
• Increase --max-iterations
  • Add clearer --proof requirements
  • Simplify the task prompt

docs.title: Review Loops
docs.text:
If agents keep requesting changes:

  • Check --review mode (try single reviewer)
  • Add explicit acceptance criteria
  • Review bridge messages for patterns

docs.title: Communication Issues
docs.text:
Check bridge status:

agentpair bridge --run-id 1

Look for:

  • Message counts (should increase over time)
  • Signal patterns (DONE/PASS/FAIL)
  • Agent distribution (both should be sending)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Single-Agent Mode — Simpler tasks
  • tmux Layout — Visual monitoring
  • Resuming Runs — Continue interrupted sessions

docs.title: Resuming Runs
docs.text: AgentPair persists run state to disk, allowing you to resume interrupted sessions.

docs.title: Run Persistence
docs.text:
Every run creates a directory:

~/.agentpair/runs/{repo-id}/{run-id}/
├── manifest.json      # Run metadata and state
├── bridge.jsonl       # Agent messages
└── transcript.jsonl   # Audit log

docs.title: Resume by Run ID
docs.text:
The simplest way to resume:

agentpair --run-id 5

This loads the run from disk and continues from the last state.

docs.title: Resume by Session ID
docs.text:
Resume using a Claude or Codex session ID:

agentpair --session abc123-def456

AgentPair searches for a run with that session ID.

docs.title: Dashboard
docs.text:
agentpair dashboard

Shows active and recent runs with their IDs.

docs.title: Bridge Command
docs.text:
agentpair bridge

Lists all runs with their IDs and status.

docs.title: Manual Lookup
docs.text: ls ~/.agentpair/runs/

docs.title: What Gets Restored
docs.text:
When resuming, AgentPair restores:

State Restored Prompt Yes Configuration Yes Iteration count Yes Loop state Yes Bridge messages Yes Agent sessions Yes (if agents support)

docs.title: 1. Start a Long Task
docs.text:
agentpair --prompt "Major refactoring" --max-iterations 50

Run ID is displayed:

INFO created run run_id=7 prompt="Major refactoring..."

docs.title: 2. Interrupt (Ctrl+C or Disconnect)
docs.text: The run state is saved automatically.

docs.title: 3. Check Status
docs.text:
agentpair bridge --run-id 7

Output:

Run #7 Bridge Status:
  Total Messages: 23
  Done Signal: false
  Pass Count: 0
  Fail Count: 0

docs.title: 4. Resume
docs.text:
agentpair --run-id 7

Continues from iteration 8 (or wherever it stopped).

docs.title: tmux and Worktree Alignment
docs.text:
When resuming runs that used --tmux or --worktree:

  • tmux: Session name stays aligned (agentpair-repo-7)
  • worktree: Same worktree is re-entered or recreated
# Original
agentpair --tmux --worktree --prompt "Task"

# Resume maintains both
agentpair --run-id 7

docs.title: Manifest Structure
docs.text:
The manifest.json contains:

{
  "id": 7,
  "repo_id": "github-user-myrepo",
  "prompt": "Major refactoring task",
  "state": "working",
  "current_iteration": 8,
  "max_iterations": 50,
  "primary_agent": "codex",
  "review_mode": "claudex",
  "done_signal": "DONE",
  "claude_session_id": "abc123",
  "codex_session_id": "def456",
  "tmux_session": "agentpair-myrepo-7",
  "worktree_path": "/home/user/.agentpair-worktree-7",
  "repo_path": "/home/user/myrepo",
  "created_at": "2024-03-28T10:00:00Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-03-28T11:30:00Z"
}

docs.title: Resuming Different States
docs.text: State On Resume submitted Start fresh working Continue primary agent reviewing Continue review completed Nothing to do failed Start fresh or fix issue

docs.title: Clearing State
docs.text:
To start fresh instead of resuming:

# Delete the run
rm -rf ~/.agentpair/runs/github-user-myrepo/7/

# Or start with new run
agentpair --prompt "Same task but fresh"

docs.title: Agent Session Resume
docs.text:
Some agents support session persistence:

Agent Session Resume Claude Via session ID Codex Via thread ID

The manifest stores these IDs for seamless resume.

docs.title: Run Not Found
docs.text:
Error: failed to load run: run not found

Check the run exists:

ls ~/.agentpair/runs/
cat ~/.agentpair/runs/*/7/manifest.json

docs.title: Corrupted State
docs.text:
If manifest is corrupted:

# View the manifest
cat ~/.agentpair/runs/github-user-myrepo/7/manifest.json | jq .

# Delete and start fresh
rm -rf ~/.agentpair/runs/github-user-myrepo/7/

docs.title: Agent Session Expired
docs.text: If agent sessions have expired, AgentPair starts new sessions but continues from the saved iteration.

docs.title: Different Directory
docs.text:
Resume works from any directory:

# Started in /home/user/project-a
agentpair --prompt "Task"

# Can resume from anywhere
cd /tmp
agentpair --run-id 7  # Uses stored repo_path

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Note your run ID — Displayed when starting
  2. Use dashboard — Easy way to find runs
  3. Check bridge status — Verify state before resuming
  4. Clean up old runs — Delete finished runs periodically

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Configuration — Default settings
  • CLI Reference — All commands and flags

docs.title: Single-Agent Mode
docs.text: For simpler tasks, run a single agent without the review loop.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Single-agent mode is ideal for:

  • Simple bug fixes
  • Documentation updates
  • Quick refactoring
  • Tasks that don't need review
  • Testing agent capabilities

docs.title: Claude Only
docs.text:
agentpair --claude-only "Fix the typo in README.md"

Or with the prompt flag:

agentpair --claude-only --prompt "Add JSDoc comments to utils.ts"

docs.title: Codex Only
docs.text: agentpair --codex-only "Update the version number to 2.0.0"

docs.title: Differences from Paired Mode
docs.text: Aspect Paired Mode Single-Agent Mode Agents 2 (worker + reviewer) 1 Review Yes No Iterations Multiple (work→review→work) Single pass Completion PASS from reviewer DONE from agent Complexity Higher overhead Lightweight

docs.title: State Machine
docs.text:
Single-agent mode uses a simplified state machine:

init → working → complete
          ↓
        failed

No reviewing state — the agent works until done or max iterations.

docs.title: Quick Fixes
docs.text:
# Fix a specific bug
agentpair --codex-only "Fix the null pointer in handleUser()"

# Update configuration
agentpair --claude-only "Change the default timeout to 30 seconds"

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
# Generate docs
agentpair --claude-only "Write API documentation for the auth module"

# Update README
agentpair --codex-only "Add installation instructions for Windows"

docs.title: Code Generation
docs.text:
# Generate boilerplate
agentpair --codex-only "Create a new React component called UserProfile"

# Add tests
agentpair --claude-only "Write unit tests for the validation functions"

docs.title: Analysis Tasks
docs.text:
# Code review
agentpair --claude-only "Review the security of the login handler"

# Performance analysis
agentpair --codex-only "Identify performance bottlenecks in the database queries"

docs.title: With Proof Requirements
docs.text:
You can still use --proof in single-agent mode:

agentpair --claude-only "Add input validation" \
  --proof "Run 'npm test' and ensure all tests pass"

The agent will verify against the proof before signaling done.

docs.title: With tmux
docs.text:
Single-agent mode works with tmux:

agentpair --claude-only --tmux "Implement caching"

Layout shows only the active agent pane.

docs.title: With Worktree
docs.text:
Isolate changes in a git worktree:

agentpair --codex-only --worktree "Experimental refactoring"

docs.title: Choosing an Agent
docs.text: Task Type Recommended Agent Code generation Codex Complex reasoning Claude Debugging Either Documentation Claude Tests Either Refactoring Claude Quick fixes Codex

docs.title: Resume
docs.text:
Single-agent runs can be resumed:

# Start a run
agentpair --claude-only "Complex task" --max-iterations 5

# Resume if interrupted
agentpair --run-id 1

The single-agent mode is preserved on resume.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use for simple tasks — Paired mode is better for complex work
  2. Set appropriate iterations — Single-agent may need fewer
  3. Include proof when needed — Helps ensure quality
  4. Watch initial runs — Verify the agent does what you expect

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Paired Sessions — For complex tasks
  • Resuming Runs — Continue interrupted sessions

docs.title: tmux Layout
docs.text: AgentPair can run agents in a tmux session with side-by-side panes for visual monitoring.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Install tmux:

macOSUbuntu/DebianFedora 
brew install tmux

sudo apt install tmux

sudo dnf install tmux

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
agentpair --tmux --prompt "Implement feature X"

This creates a detached tmux session with the layout:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                          │
│   Claude Pane (0)          │    Codex Pane (1)          │
│                            │                            │
│   [Claude output here]     │    [Codex output here]     │
│                            │                            │
│                            │                            │
│                            │                            │
├────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────┤
│   Status Pane (2)                                        │
│   [Bridge status, iteration info]                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Attaching to Sessions
docs.text:
After starting:

# Attach to the session
tmux attach -t agentpair-myrepo-1

Session names follow the pattern: agentpair-{repo}-{run-id}

docs.title: List Sessions
docs.text:
tmux list-sessions

Output:

agentpair-myrepo-1: 3 windows (created Sat Mar 28 10:00:00 2024)
agentpair-myrepo-2: 3 windows (created Sat Mar 28 11:30:00 2024)

docs.title: Detach
docs.text: While attached, press Ctrl+B then D to detach.

docs.title: Kill Session
docs.text: tmux kill-session -t agentpair-myrepo-1

docs.title: Pane Navigation
docs.text:
Standard tmux navigation:

Keys Action Ctrl+B ← Move to left pane Ctrl+B → Move to right pane Ctrl+B ↑ Move to upper pane Ctrl+B ↓ Move to lower pane Ctrl+B z Zoom current pane Ctrl+B [ Enter scroll mode q Exit scroll mode

docs.title: Scrolling
docs.text:
To scroll through agent output:

  1. Press Ctrl+B then [ to enter scroll mode
  2. Use arrow keys or Page Up/Down to scroll
  3. Press q to exit scroll mode

docs.title: Layout Customization
docs.text:
The default layout uses equal horizontal splits for agents and a smaller bottom pane for status.

tmux configuration can be customized in ~/.tmux.conf:

# Example: increase scrollback
set-option -g history-limit 50000

# Mouse support for scrolling
set -g mouse on

docs.title: Persistent Sessions
docs.text:
tmux sessions persist even if:

  • You close your terminal
  • You disconnect from SSH
  • Your shell exits

Reattach anytime with:

tmux attach -t agentpair-myrepo-1

docs.title: With SSH
docs.text:
tmux is particularly useful over SSH:

# SSH into remote machine
ssh user@remote

# Start AgentPair with tmux
agentpair --tmux --prompt "Task"

# Detach (Ctrl+B, D)

# Disconnect SSH safely

# Later, reconnect
ssh user@remote
tmux attach -t agentpair-myrepo-1

docs.title: With Worktree
docs.text:
Combine tmux with worktree isolation:

agentpair --tmux --worktree --prompt "Experimental feature"

docs.title: Example Workflow
docs.text:
1. Start session: 

    agentpair --tmux --prompt "Implement OAuth login"

  2. Attach to watch: 

    tmux attach -t agentpair-myproject-1

  3. Navigate panes:

  4. Ctrl+B → to see Codex
  5. Ctrl+B ← to see Claude

  6. Ctrl+B ↓ to see status

  7. Scroll history:

  8. Ctrl+B [ to enter scroll mode
  9. Page Up/Down to navigate

  10. q to exit

  11. Detach when satisfied:

  12. Ctrl+B D to detach

  13. Check status later: 

    agentpair dashboard
agentpair bridge --run-id 1

docs.title: "tmux not available"
docs.text: Install tmux (see Prerequisites above).

docs.title: Session Not Found
docs.text:
# List all sessions
tmux list-sessions

# Session might have completed
agentpair dashboard

docs.title: Panes Not Updating
docs.text:
• Check if agents are still running
  • View bridge status: agentpair bridge --run-id 1
  • Check for errors in agent panes

docs.title: Terminal Issues
docs.text:
If the terminal looks corrupted:

# Reset terminal
reset

# Or restart tmux server
tmux kill-server

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Git Worktrees — Isolate changes
  • Resuming Runs — Continue sessions

docs.title: Git Worktrees
docs.text: AgentPair can create isolated git worktrees for each run, keeping your main branch clean.

docs.title: What is a Worktree?
docs.text:
A git worktree is a linked working directory that shares the same repository history but has its own working tree and index. Changes in the worktree don't affect your main checkout until merged.

my-repo/                     # Main checkout (main branch)
├── .git/
├── src/
└── ...

.agentpair-worktree-1/       # Worktree (agentpair/run-1 branch)
├── .git                     # File pointing to main .git
├── src/                     # Separate working tree
└── ...

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Git repository (initialized)
  • At least one commit in the repository

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
agentpair --worktree --prompt "Experimental feature X"

AgentPair will:

  1. Create a new branch: agentpair/run-{id}
  2. Create a worktree: .agentpair-worktree-{id} (in parent directory)
  3. Run agents in the worktree
  4. Clean up worktree on completion (branch remains)

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
After creating a worktree:

/home/user/projects/
├── my-repo/                        # Your main checkout
│   ├── .git/
│   │   └── worktrees/
│   │       └── .agentpair-worktree-1/
│   └── src/
└── .agentpair-worktree-1/          # AgentPair worktree
    ├── .git                        # Points to main .git
    └── src/                        # Isolated working tree

docs.title: Branch Naming
docs.text: Run ID Branch Name 1 agentpair/run-1 2 agentpair/run-2 42 agentpair/run-42

docs.title: 1. Start with Worktree
docs.text:
cd ~/projects/my-repo
agentpair --worktree --prompt "Add new authentication system"

docs.title: 2. Agents Work in Isolation
docs.text: All changes happen in .agentpair-worktree-1/, not your main checkout.

docs.title: 3. Review Changes
docs.text:
After completion, review the branch:

# See the branch
git branch -a | grep agentpair

# Check out the branch
git checkout agentpair/run-1

# Or view diff
git diff main..agentpair/run-1

docs.title: 4. Merge If Satisfied
docs.text:
git checkout main
git merge agentpair/run-1

# Or create a PR
git push origin agentpair/run-1
gh pr create --base main --head agentpair/run-1

docs.title: 5. Cleanup
docs.text:
The worktree is automatically removed on completion. To clean up the branch:

git branch -d agentpair/run-1

docs.title: With tmux
docs.text:
Combine worktree with tmux:

agentpair --worktree --tmux --prompt "Risky refactoring"

docs.title: List Worktrees
docs.text:
git worktree list

Output:

/home/user/my-repo                  abc1234 [main]
/home/user/.agentpair-worktree-1    def5678 [agentpair/run-1]

docs.title: Remove Worktree Manually
docs.text:
If a worktree wasn't cleaned up:

git worktree remove .agentpair-worktree-1

docs.title: Prune Stale Worktrees
docs.text: git worktree prune

docs.title: Experimental Features
docs.text:
agentpair --worktree --prompt "Try implementing feature with new architecture"

If it works: merge. If not: delete the branch.

docs.title: Parallel Development
docs.text:
Run multiple AgentPair sessions on different features:

# Terminal 1
agentpair --worktree --prompt "Add user management"

# Terminal 2
agentpair --worktree --prompt "Add payment integration"

Each gets its own worktree and branch.

docs.title: Safe Refactoring
docs.text:
agentpair --worktree --prompt "Refactor database layer" \
  --proof "All tests must pass"

Main branch stays untouched until you're satisfied.

docs.title: Resuming with Worktree
docs.text:
When resuming a run that used worktree:

agentpair --run-id 1

AgentPair will:

  1. Check if the worktree still exists
  2. Re-enter or recreate the matching worktree
  3. Continue work on the same branch

docs.title: "Not a git repository"
docs.text:
Worktree requires an initialized git repo:

git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit"

docs.title: Worktree Already Exists
docs.text:
If a worktree wasn't cleaned up:

# Remove the stale worktree
git worktree remove .agentpair-worktree-1

# Or force remove
git worktree remove --force .agentpair-worktree-1

docs.title: Branch Conflicts
docs.text:
If the branch name already exists:

# Delete the old branch
git branch -D agentpair/run-1

# Then retry
agentpair --worktree --prompt "Task"

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use for risky changes — Worktrees isolate experimental work
  2. Review before merging — Check the branch thoroughly
  3. Clean up branches — Delete merged or abandoned branches
  4. Combine with proof — Ensure tests pass before considering merge

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Resuming Runs — Continue interrupted sessions
  • tmux Layout — Visual monitoring

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: Complete command-line reference for AgentPair.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: agentpair [command] [flags] [prompt]

docs.title: Main Command
docs.text:
agentpair [flags] [prompt]

Run a paired or single-agent session.

docs.title: dashboard
docs.text:
agentpair dashboard

Show live dashboard of active runs.

docs.title: bridge
docs.text:
agentpair bridge [--run-id ID]

Show bridge status for runs.

docs.title: update
docs.text:
agentpair update

Check for and install updates.

docs.title: version
docs.text:
agentpair version

Print version information.

docs.title: Task Configuration
docs.text: Flag Short Default Description --prompt -p Task prompt (or provide as positional argument) --proof Proof/verification command for task completion --done DONE Custom done signal to look for

docs.title: Agent Configuration
docs.text: Flag Short Default Description --agent -a codex Primary worker agent (claude or codex) --review claudex Review mode (claude, codex, or claudex) --claude-only false Run Claude in single-agent mode --codex-only false Run Codex in single-agent mode

docs.title: Loop Configuration
docs.text: Flag Short Default Description --max-iterations -m 20 Maximum loop iterations before stopping

docs.title: Workspace Configuration
docs.text: Flag Short Default Description --tmux false Use tmux for side-by-side panes --worktree false Create git worktree for isolation

docs.title: Resume Configuration
docs.text: Flag Short Default Description --run-id 0 Resume by run ID --session Resume by session ID (Claude or Codex)

docs.title: Output Configuration
docs.text: Flag Short Default Description --verbose -v false Enable verbose output

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
# Paired session with prompt as argument
agentpair "Implement user authentication"

# Paired session with --prompt flag
agentpair --prompt "Implement user authentication"

# Single-agent mode
agentpair --claude-only "Fix the bug in login.go"
agentpair --codex-only "Add unit tests"

docs.title: Agent Selection
docs.text:
# Claude as primary worker (Codex reviews)
agentpair --agent claude --prompt "Complex reasoning task"

# Codex as primary worker (Claude reviews) - default
agentpair --agent codex --prompt "Code generation task"

docs.title: Review Modes
docs.text:
# Only Claude reviews
agentpair --review claude --prompt "Task"

# Only Codex reviews
agentpair --review codex --prompt "Task"

# Both review (default)
agentpair --review claudex --prompt "Task"

docs.title: Proof Requirements
docs.text:
# With test verification
agentpair --prompt "Add validation" --proof "go test ./..."

# With multiple checks
agentpair --prompt "Security fix" --proof "go test ./... && golangci-lint run"

docs.title: Iteration Control
docs.text:
# Increase iterations for complex tasks
agentpair --max-iterations 50 --prompt "Major refactoring"

# Limit iterations for quick tasks
agentpair --max-iterations 5 --prompt "Simple fix"

docs.title: Workspace Options
docs.text:
# With tmux side-by-side view
agentpair --tmux --prompt "Task"

# With git worktree isolation
agentpair --worktree --prompt "Experimental feature"

# Both
agentpair --tmux --worktree --prompt "Risky changes"

docs.title: Resuming
docs.text:
# Resume by run ID
agentpair --run-id 7

# Resume by session ID
agentpair --session abc123-def456

docs.title: Combined Options
docs.text:
# Full-featured run
agentpair --tmux --worktree \
  --agent claude \
  --review claudex \
  --max-iterations 30 \
  --proof "npm test && npm run lint" \
  --verbose \
  --prompt "Implement OAuth2 with Google"

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text: Code Meaning 0 Success 1 Error (see stderr for details)

docs.title: Environment
docs.text: AgentPair does not currently read environment variables. Use the config file or CLI flags.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: Path Purpose ~/.agentpair/config.yaml Global configuration ~/.agentpair/runs/ Run persistence

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Configuration — Config file reference
  • Bridge Messages — Message format reference

docs.title: Config File Reference
docs.text: AgentPair configuration file specification.

docs.title: Location
docs.text:
Config files are loaded from ~/.agentpair/ in this order:

  1. config.yaml (preferred)
  2. config.yml
  3. config.json

Only the first found file is loaded.

docs.title: YAML (Recommended)
docs.text:
# ~/.agentpair/config.yaml

# Primary worker agent: claude or codex
agent: codex

# Maximum loop iterations before stopping
max_iterations: 20

# Proof/verification command
proof: ""

# Review mode: claude, codex, or claudex
review_mode: claudex

# Custom done signal
done_signal: DONE

# Enable tmux side-by-side view
use_tmux: false

# Enable git worktree isolation
use_worktree: false

# Enable verbose logging
verbose: false

# Maximum run duration (Go duration format)
timeout: 2h

docs.title: JSON
docs.text:
{
  "agent": "codex",
  "max_iterations": 20,
  "proof": "",
  "review_mode": "claudex",
  "done_signal": "DONE",
  "use_tmux": false,
  "use_worktree": false,
  "verbose": false,
  "timeout": "2h"
}

docs.title: agent
docs.text:
Type: string Default: codex Values: claude, codex

Primary worker agent. The other agent acts as reviewer.

agent: claude

docs.title: max_iterations
docs.text:
Type: integer Default: 20 Range: 1-1000

Maximum loop iterations before stopping.

max_iterations: 50

docs.title: proof
docs.text:
Type: string Default: (empty)

Command to verify task completion. Passed to agents.

proof: "go test ./... && golangci-lint run"

docs.title: review_mode
docs.text:
Type: string Default: claudex Values: claude, codex, claudex

Who reviews the work:

  • claude — Only Claude reviews
  • codex — Only Codex reviews
  • claudex — Both review; consensus required
review_mode: claudex

docs.title: done_signal
docs.text:
Type: string Default: DONE

Signal agents output to indicate task completion.

done_signal: "COMPLETE"

docs.title: use_tmux
docs.text:
Type: boolean Default: false

Enable tmux side-by-side panes.

use_tmux: true

docs.title: use_worktree
docs.text:
Type: boolean Default: false

Create git worktree for run isolation.

use_worktree: true

docs.title: verbose
docs.text:
Type: boolean Default: false

Enable verbose logging output.

verbose: true

docs.title: timeout
docs.text:
Type: string (duration) Default: 2h

Maximum run duration. Uses Go duration format:

  • 30m — 30 minutes
  • 2h — 2 hours
  • 1h30m — 1 hour 30 minutes
timeout: 4h

docs.title: Precedence
docs.text:
CLI flags override config file values:

# Config says agent: codex
# CLI overrides to claude
agentpair --agent claude --prompt "Task"

docs.title: Development
docs.text:
# Fast feedback, visual monitoring
agent: codex
max_iterations: 10
use_tmux: true
verbose: false

docs.title: CI/CD
docs.text:
# Long-running, thorough
agent: codex
max_iterations: 100
review_mode: claudex
verbose: true
timeout: 8h

docs.title: Safety-Focused
docs.text:
# Isolated, verified
agent: claude
max_iterations: 20
use_worktree: true
proof: "npm test && npm run lint"
review_mode: claudex

docs.title: Minimal
docs.text:
# Accept all defaults except iterations
max_iterations: 30

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
AgentPair validates config values:

Field Validation agent Must be claude or codex max_iterations Must be positive integer review_mode Must be claude, codex, or claudex timeout Must be valid Go duration

Invalid values cause startup errors.

docs.title: Creating Config
docs.text:
Create with your preferred editor:

mkdir -p ~/.agentpair
cat > ~/.agentpair/config.yaml << 'EOF'
agent: codex
max_iterations: 20
review_mode: claudex
EOF

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• CLI Reference — Command-line flags
  • Configuration Guide — Usage examples

docs.title: Bridge Messages Reference
docs.text: Complete specification for bridge message format.

docs.title: Message Structure
docs.text:
Each message is a JSON object in the bridge JSONL file:

{
  "id": "sha256-hash-of-content",
  "run_id": 1,
  "type": "task",
  "from": "claude",
  "to": "codex",
  "content": "Message content here",
  "signal": "",
  "timestamp": "2024-03-28T10:00:00Z"
}

docs.title: id
docs.text:
Type: string Format: SHA256 hex string

Unique message identifier generated from content hash. Used for deduplication.

"id": "a1b2c3d4e5f6..."

docs.title: run_id
docs.text:
Type: integer

Associated run ID.

"run_id": 7

docs.title: type
docs.text:
Type: string Values: task, result, review, signal, chat

Message type (see Message Types below).

"type": "task"

docs.title: from
docs.text:
Type: string

Sender agent name.

"from": "claude"

docs.title: to
docs.text:
Type: string

Target agent name. Empty for broadcast messages.

"to": "codex"

docs.title: content
docs.text:
Type: string

Message content. Can be any text including markdown.

"content": "Please implement the login validation..."

docs.title: signal
docs.text:
Type: string Values: DONE, PASS, FAIL, or empty

Control signal for signal type messages.

"signal": "PASS"

docs.title: timestamp
docs.text:
Type: string Format: ISO 8601

Message creation time.

"timestamp": "2024-03-28T10:00:00Z"

docs.title: task
docs.text:
Work request or follow-up task.

{
  "type": "task",
  "from": "claude",
  "to": "codex",
  "content": "Please implement input validation for the email field. Requirements:\n1. Check for valid email format\n2. Show error message for invalid input\n3. Disable submit button until valid"
}

docs.title: result
docs.text:
Work output from an agent.

{
  "type": "result",
  "from": "codex",
  "to": "claude",
  "content": "Implemented email validation:

```typescript
function validateEmail(email: string): boolean {
  return /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/.test(email);
}
```

Added to LoginForm component with error state management."
}

docs.title: review
docs.text:
Review feedback.

{
  "type": "review",
  "from": "claude",
  "to": "codex",
  "content": "The implementation looks good. A few suggestions:\n\n1. Consider using a more comprehensive email regex\n2. Add aria-label for accessibility\n3. Consider debouncing the validation\n\nPlease address these points."
}

docs.title: signal
docs.text:
Control signal for loop state.

{
  "type": "signal",
  "from": "claude",
  "to": "",
  "content": "All requirements met, validation working correctly",
  "signal": "PASS"
}

docs.title: chat
docs.text:
Free-form discussion or clarification.

{
  "type": "chat",
  "from": "codex",
  "to": "claude",
  "content": "Should we also validate the password field in this PR, or keep it separate?"
}

docs.title: DONE
docs.text:
Agent has completed its work.

{
  "type": "signal",
  "signal": "DONE",
  "content": "Task completed successfully"
}

docs.title: PASS
docs.text:
Reviewer approves the work.

{
  "type": "signal",
  "signal": "PASS",
  "content": "All requirements met, code looks good"
}

docs.title: FAIL
docs.text:
Reviewer rejects the work.

{
  "type": "signal",
  "signal": "FAIL",
  "content": "Tests are failing, please fix before proceeding"
}

docs.title: ID Generation
docs.text:
Message IDs are SHA256 hashes:

func GenerateID(msgType MessageType, from, to, content string) string {
    h := sha256.New()
    h.Write([]byte(string(msgType)))
    h.Write([]byte(from))
    h.Write([]byte(to))
    h.Write([]byte(content))
    return hex.EncodeToString(h.Sum(nil))
}

Same content always generates the same ID, enabling deduplication.

docs.title: Storage Format
docs.text:
Messages are stored in JSONL (newline-delimited JSON):

{"id":"abc...","type":"task","from":"claude","to":"codex","content":"...","timestamp":"..."}
{"id":"def...","type":"result","from":"codex","to":"claude","content":"...","timestamp":"..."}
{"id":"ghi...","type":"review","from":"claude","to":"codex","content":"...","timestamp":"..."}

File location: ~/.agentpair/runs/{repo-id}/{run-id}/bridge.jsonl

docs.title: Example Conversation
docs.text:
Complete message flow for a simple task:

// 1. Claude assigns task
{"type":"task","from":"claude","to":"codex","content":"Add email validation to LoginForm","timestamp":"2024-03-28T10:00:00Z"}

// 2. Codex implements
{"type":"result","from":"codex","to":"claude","content":"Added validateEmail function...","timestamp":"2024-03-28T10:01:00Z"}

// 3. Claude reviews with feedback
{"type":"review","from":"claude","to":"codex","content":"Please add error message display","timestamp":"2024-03-28T10:02:00Z"}

// 4. Codex fixes
{"type":"result","from":"codex","to":"claude","content":"Added error message UI...","timestamp":"2024-03-28T10:03:00Z"}

// 5. Codex signals done
{"type":"signal","from":"codex","signal":"DONE","content":"Validation complete","timestamp":"2024-03-28T10:03:30Z"}

// 6. Claude approves
{"type":"signal","from":"claude","signal":"PASS","content":"Looks good","timestamp":"2024-03-28T10:04:00Z"}

docs.title: MCP Tool Mapping
docs.text:
Bridge messages are accessed via MCP tools:

MCP Tool Bridge Operation send_to_agent Append message to JSONL receive_messages Read messages for agent bridge_status Count messages and signals

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Bridge Concept — How the bridge works
  • MCP Integration — Tool access

docs.title: v0.1.0
docs.text:
Released: 2026-03-28

Initial release of AgentPair - a Go implementation of agent-to-agent pair programming between Claude and Codex.

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text:
• 🤝 Paired Execution — Claude and Codex work together with one implementing and the other reviewing
  • 🌉 Bridge Communication — JSONL-based messaging with SHA256 deduplication and MCP tool integration
  • 🖥️ Workspace Support — tmux side-by-side panes and git worktree isolation for clean development

docs.title: Agent Orchestration
docs.text:
AgentPair introduces a state machine-based orchestration loop that coordinates between Claude and Codex agents:

init → working → reviewing → working → ... → complete
  • Paired mode (default): One agent works while the other reviews
  • Single-agent mode: Run with --claude-only or --codex-only for simpler tasks
  • Review modes: Choose claude, codex, or claudex (consensus required)

docs.title: Bridge Communication
docs.text:
Agents communicate through a JSONL bridge file with MCP tools:

  • send_to_agent — Send messages to the other agent
  • receive_messages — Get pending messages
  • bridge_status — Check bridge state

Message types include: task, result, review, signal, and chat.

docs.title: Run Persistence
docs.text:
Every run is persisted to ~/.agentpair/runs/:

~/.agentpair/runs/{repo-id}/{run-id}/
├── manifest.json      # Run metadata
├── bridge.jsonl       # Agent messages
└── transcript.jsonl   # Audit log

Resume interrupted runs with --run-id or --session.

docs.title: Workspace Features
docs.text:
• tmux support — Side-by-side terminal panes with --tmux
  • Git worktrees — Isolated environments with --worktree
  • Live dashboard — Monitor active runs with agentpair dashboard

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
go install github.com/plexusone/agentpair@v0.1.0

Or build from source:

git clone https://github.com/plexusone/agentpair
cd agentpair
git checkout v0.1.0
go build -o agentpair ./cmd/agentpair

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Paired session (Codex works, Claude reviews)
agentpair --prompt "Implement user authentication"

# With tmux side-by-side view
agentpair --tmux --prompt "Add OAuth2 support"

# Single-agent mode
agentpair --claude-only "Fix the bug in login.go"

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Create ~/.agentpair/config.yaml:

agent: codex
max_iterations: 20
review_mode: claudex
use_tmux: false
use_worktree: false

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: Package Version Purpose github.com/spf13/cobra v1.10.2 CLI framework github.com/coder/websocket v1.8.14 WebSocket client/server github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk v1.4.1 MCP integration go.yaml.in/yaml/v3 v3.0.4 YAML configuration

docs.title: Test Coverage
docs.text: Package Status pkg/jsonl ✓ internal/bridge ✓ internal/config ✓ internal/logger ✓ internal/loop ✓ internal/review ✓ internal/run ✓ internal/tmux ✓ internal/worktree ✓

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• Claude and Codex CLIs must be installed and authenticated separately
  • Auto-approve mode is enabled by default (run in VM/container for safety)
  • Integration tests with real agents are not included in this release

docs.title: Acknowledgments
docs.text:
AgentPair is inspired by loop, a TypeScript implementation of the same concept. This Go port provides:

  • Native binary distribution (no Node.js required)
  • Structured logging via slog
  • MCP integration via official Go SDK
  • Comprehensive test coverage
